question.text: The concept of "beneficial ownership" in the context of DTAA means that:

answer.text: Any entity receiving income from a treaty country can claim DTAA benefits regardless of its role
The country offering the lower tax rate is considered the beneficial owner of the treaty
DTAA benefits are available only to the beneficial owner of the income, not to conduit or intermediary entities
The concept applies only to individual taxpayers and not to corporate entities

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The beneficial ownership test ensures that DTAA benefits are claimed only by the true economic owner of the income, not by conduit entities or intermediaries set up solely to exploit treaty benefits (treaty shopping). If an entity is merely a pass-through with no real economic substance, it cannot claim to be the beneficial owner. **The Trap:** The idea that "any entity receiving income can claim benefits" is precisely what the beneficial ownership test prevents. The concept applies to all entities, not just individuals. **Cross-Exam Context:** Beneficial ownership is tested in NISM Series X-B in the context of anti-avoidance provisions and is also relevant to GAAR (General Anti-Avoidance Rule) concepts. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When structuring investments through foreign entities, ensure the entity has genuine economic substance — hollow shell structures will fail the beneficial ownership test and lose DTAA benefits.
